Some people from Judea taught the Gentile converts at Antioch that they could not be saved unless they observed the whole ceremonial law as given by Moses; and in this way they tried to destroy Christian freedom. There is a tendency in us to think that everyone who does not do things exactly as we do is wrong. Their teaching was very discouraging. Wise and good people desire to avoid arguments and disagreements as much as possible; yet when false teachers oppose the main truths of the gospel or introduce harmful teachings, we must not hesitate to oppose them.

We see from the words “purifying their hearts by faith,” and the address of St. Peter, that justification by faith and sanctification by the Holy Spirit cannot be separated; and that both are gifts from God. We have great reason to thank God for hearing the gospel. May we have that faith which the one who searches our hearts approves and confirms with the seal of the Holy Spirit. Then our hearts and consciences will be purified from the guilt of sin, and we will be freed from the burdens some try to place on Christ’s followers. Paul and Barnabas showed by clear examples that God accepted the preaching of the pure gospel to the Gentiles without the law of Moses; therefore, insisting on that law would undo what God had done. James' opinion was that Gentile converts should not worry about Jewish customs, but they should abstain from foods offered to idols so that they could show their hatred of idolatry. Also, they should be warned against sexual immorality, which the Gentiles did not abhor as it should be and even included in some of their rituals. They were advised to abstain from things strangled and from eating blood; this was forbidden by the law of Moses and also here, out of reverence for the blood of the sacrifices, which were still being offered, so that it would needlessly grieve Jewish converts and further prejudice unconverted Jews. But since the reason has long ceased, we are free in this matter, as in others. Let new believers be warned to avoid all appearances of the evils they formerly practiced or are likely to be tempted by; and let them use Christian freedom with moderation and caution.

Being assured that they were directed by the immediate influence of the Holy Spirit, the apostles and disciples felt it was pleasing to God the Holy Spirit as well as to themselves to place no other burden on the converts than the things mentioned earlier, which were necessary either for their own sake or because of current circumstances. It was a comfort to hear that external rituals were no longer imposed, which troubled consciences but could not purify or pacify them; and those who caused such trouble were silenced so that peace in the church was restored and what threatened division was removed. All this brought consolation for which they thanked God. Many others were at Antioch. Where many people work in the word and teaching, there is still opportunity for us: the zeal and usefulness of others should encourage us, not discourage us.

Here we have a private disagreement between two ministers, Paul and Barnabas, but it ended well. Barnabas wanted his nephew John Mark to go with them. We should suspect ourselves of being biased and guard against this when promoting relatives. Paul did not think him worthy of the honor or fit for the service because he had left them without their knowledge or consent: see ch. Neither would give in, so there was no alternative but they must separate. We see that even the best people are only human, subject to the same emotions as we are. Perhaps both sides were at fault, as is usual in such disagreements. Christ’s example alone is a perfect model. Yet we should not be surprised if there are differences among wise and good people. This will continue while we are in this imperfect state; we will never all agree until we reach heaven. But what harm the remnants of pride and passion do, even in good people, in the world and in the church! Many who lived at Antioch, who had heard little about the devotion and piety of Paul and Barnabas, heard about their disagreement and separation; and this is how it will be with us if we give way to arguments. Believers must pray consistently so that they are never led by uncontrolled tempers to harm the cause they truly want to serve. Paul speaks with respect and affection for both Barnabas and Mark in his letters, written after this event. May all who profess your name, O loving Savior, be thoroughly reconciled by that love derived from you which is not easily provoked and which quickly forgets and buries offenses.
